response:
Since this involves retirement account strategy and tax implications, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
The mega backdoor Roth is a powerful strategy, but whether it's right for you depends on a few key factors. Let me walk you through the decision framework.
What it actually is
A mega backdoor Roth lets you contribute far more to a Roth account than the standard $7,500 IRA limit allows. In 2026, the total 401(k) plan limit under Section 415(c) is $72,000. Once you've maxed your regular $24,500 elective deferral and your employer has contributed their match, any remaining "headroom" up to $72,000 can potentially be filled with after-tax (non-Roth) contributions — and then immediately converted to Roth. The result: tax-free growth on a much larger pool of money.
The three gates your plan must clear
This strategy only works if your Microsoft 401(k) plan allows all three of the following:
After-tax (non-Roth) voluntary contributions — distinct from your regular Roth 401(k) deferrals
In-plan Roth conversions or in-service distributions to roll to a Roth IRA
Access to those features while still employed — not just upon separation
Microsoft is actually one of the more generous employers in this regard, and many Microsoft employees do have access to mega backdoor Roth. But you'll want to verify by checking your Summary Plan Description (SPD) or calling Fidelity/Vanguard (whichever holds your plan).
The case FOR doing it
No income limits — unlike a direct Roth IRA (which phases out for single filers between $153,000–$168,000 MAGI in 2026), the mega backdoor Roth has no income cap
Massive scale — potential to shelter tens of thousands more per year in tax-free growth
No RMDs — Roth accounts have no required minimum distributions during your lifetime, ideal for long-term compounding
Tax diversification — having both pre-tax and Roth buckets gives you flexibility in retirement to manage your tax bracket year by year
The case AGAINST (or reasons to pause)
No immediate tax break — after-tax contributions don't reduce your 2026 taxable income the way traditional 401(k) dollars do
Earnings timing risk — if you don't convert quickly, gains in the after-tax sub-account become taxable at conversion. Many plans now offer automatic daily sweeps to mitigate this
ACP nondiscrimination testing — if too few lower-paid employees participate, the plan may force "highly compensated employees" (those earning over $160,000) to receive corrective distributions, unwinding the strategy
Complexity — requires tracking basis carefully, especially if you also have a rollover IRA
A quick capacity example
If you max your $24,500 elective deferral and Microsoft contributes, say, $10,000 in matching contributions, your after-tax mega backdoor capacity could be up to $37,500 ($72,000 minus $24,500 minus $10,000). That's a substantial Roth opportunity above and beyond standard limits.
Bottom line
For a high-income earner over the Roth IRA income threshold, the mega backdoor Roth is one of the most effective tax-free wealth-building tools available — if your plan supports it. The first step is simply confirming whether your Microsoft 401(k) plan document permits after-tax contributions and in-service conversions.
Would you like help thinking through how much after-tax contribution capacity you might have once we factor in your income and expected employer match?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.

-$20K
